title: Feathered Neighbors: Peacocks Divide a California Town
summary: When you think of an "invasive species," you probably imagine zebra mussels or kudzu vine—organisms that cause billions of dollars in damage each year. But what about peacocks? In Arcadia, California, a city about 45 minutes from downtown Los Angeles, dozens of these vibrant birds have become a controversial part of daily life. They meander through yards, traipse across rooftops, peck at cars, and call loudly from trees. During mating season, the males display their spectacular, iridescent feathers. But not everyone is charmed. The peacocks were introduced more than a century ago by Elias Lucky Baldwin, a businessman and land baron. Back then, the area was open land, but over the years it filled with homes and commercial strips. Now, the birds are protected by local laws, leading to an overpopulation problem that divides the community. Some residents adore the peacocks and want to protect them, while others see them as a nuisance and would prefer relocation programs. Filmmaker Callie Barlow explores this tension in her short documentary "Our Neighbors, the Peacocks." The film doesn't offer a neat resolution. Instead, as Jason Sondhi of Short of the Week notes, "it leans into a modest but resonant idea articulated by its director that living alongside these animals might require 'putting aside your own discomforts to find a deeper meaning in nature.'" Barlow's film highlights how a species introduced for beauty can become a source of conflict, forcing a community to ask: What does it mean to coexist with wildlife?
